refined_fr.refined_fr: Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model.
refined_fr.rationale: The candidate FR conflates three distinct functional obligations: (1) terrain analysis, (2) obstacle identification, and (3) work-zone representation. Each is a separate concern with different data sources, processing logic, and stakeholder consumption patterns. The refined FR is solution-neutral (avoids 'mapping/modeling' terminology which implies implementation choice) and focuses on the core problem: making worksite geometry available to downstream planning and execution functions. Splitting into atomic sub-FRs allows independent verification and design iteration.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verify that a worksite spatial model exists in a defined format (e.g., point cloud, mesh, or feature set) and can be queried by planning/execution functions to retrieve feature geometry and attributes within specified accuracy tolerances.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire terrain elevation and surface geometry data from the worksite.
FR-NGHE-006.2: Identify and classify static obstacles (e.g., structures, vegetation, utilities) within the worksite boundary.
FR-NGHE-006.3: Define and represent work-zone boundaries and exclusion zones per operational policy.
FR-NGHE-006.4: Integrate terrain, obstacle, and work-zone data into a unified spatial model accessible to planning and execution functions.
refined_fr.note: The parent FR (CR-NGHE-006) remains composite; the sub-FRs are atomic and ready for independent DP and IM assignment.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire spatial feature data via multi-modal sensing (vision, range, positioning) and store in a unified geometric representation (point clouds, meshes, or voxel grids) with feature classification metadata; expose via standardized query interface.

DP2: Decompose worksite into logical zones (safety perimeters, operational areas, hazard regions) and represent each zone as a parameterized geometric primitive (polygon, polyhedron) with associated feature attributes; maintain zone registry and spatial index.

DP3: Ingest pre-existing site documentation (CAD, survey data, safety plans) as authoritative baseline; overlay real-time observations as delta layers; reconcile conflicts via confidence scoring and temporal versioning.

DP4: Implement a feature-agnostic spatial database (graph or relational) that stores feature observations as tuples of (geometry, class, confidence, timestamp, source); provide query API for retrieval by spatial bounds, feature type, or temporal range.

DP5: Establish a modular feature extraction pipeline: raw sensor data → preprocessing → feature detection → classification → spatial registration; each stage produces intermediate artifacts consumable by downstream planning/execution functions.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP4 + DP5 (hybrid): Implement a feature-agnostic spatial database that stores observations as (geometry, class, confidence, timestamp, source) tuples, fed by a modular feature extraction pipeline. This decouples sensor acquisition and processing (DP5) from the representation and query layer (DP4), allowing independent evolution of sensing strategies and planning/execution consumers.

potential_sources: Tight coupling between feature classification confidence thresholds and planning/execution acceptance criteria—mitigated by explicit confidence metadata in tuples.
Dependency on spatial registration accuracy (coordinate frame alignment)—mitigated by versioning and source attribution.
Pipeline stage interdependencies (e.g., preprocessing output format constrains feature detection input)—mitigated by defining intermediate artifact schemas.
Query API contract with planning/execution functions—mitigated by designing API to be feature-type and geometry-agnostic.
coupling_check.notes: DP4+DP5 avoids premature commitment to specific sensing modalities or zone decomposition strategies (DP1, DP2). The tuple-based schema is neutral with respect to feature types, allowing safety plan and operational policies to define which features are captured without redesigning the core representation. Temporal versioning and source tracking support traceability required by safety-critical workflows.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Planning/execution functions can tolerate asynchronous, eventually-consistent spatial data (not real-time streaming). RISK: If latency requirements are sub-second, DP4+DP5 may require in-memory caching and event-driven updates.

ASSUMPTION: Feature classification confidence scores are meaningful and calibrated. RISK: If confidence metrics are poorly defined or uncalibrated, planning/execution functions may make unsafe decisions; recommend explicit acceptance criteria tied to confidence thresholds.

ASSUMPTION: Spatial registration (coordinate frame alignment) is solved independently or provided by external localization service. RISK: If registration drift accumulates, spatial queries become unreliable; recommend periodic re-registration and drift monitoring.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan and operational policies are stable or change infrequently. RISK: If policies change dynamically, feature capture and representation may need to adapt; recommend policy versioning and feature schema evolution strategy.

ASSUMPTION: Multiple sensing modalities (if used) can be fused into a common coordinate frame. RISK: Sensor calibration and synchronization errors propagate into spatial model; recommend sensor fusion validation and uncertainty quantification.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) Required feature types (e.g., obstacles, hazard zones, access points, equipment locations) derived from safety plan and operational policies; (2) Acceptable confidence/accuracy thresholds for each feature type; (3) Latency and update frequency requirements for planning/execution consumers; (4) Coordinate frame and spatial resolution requirements. This will enable tighter DP specification and IM boundary definition (e.g., whether feature extraction is centralized or distributed across edge devices).

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized spatial observation database and feature extraction pipeline that decouples sensor data ingestion from planning/execution consumers. Owns the tuple schema (geometry, class, confidence, timestamp, source), the modular feature extraction framework, and query interfaces for downstream planning and safety services.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Raw sensor observations (geometry, raw features, timestamps, source identifiers) flow from Vehicle and Teleoperation into the feature extraction pipeline.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Processed spatial observations (geometry, class, confidence, timestamp, source) are queried by planning and execution modules to inform task decisions.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: High-confidence hazard observations (e.g., obstacles, no-go zones) are published to Safety Services for constraint enforcement.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Distributed feature extraction pipelines may ingest remote sensor data or publish observations to remote planning nodes; network link mediates data flow for multi-agent scenarios.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. NGHE Solution is the natural home for the spatial database and feature extraction pipeline—it is the planning/perception backbone. The DP4 (representation/query layer) and DP5 (modular feature extraction) are both core to NGHE's mission of autonomous decision-making. Minimal diffusion: only Telemetry (in), Tasking (out), SafetyAlert (out), and Network Link (inout) cross the boundary. Internal cohesion is high because feature extraction and spatial queries are tightly coupled in the planning loop.

Poor fit. Vehicle is a hardware/actuation module, not a perception/planning module. Placing the spatial database and feature extraction pipeline in Vehicle would create severe coupling: planning logic would depend on Vehicle internals, and feature extraction would be tightly bound to a single hardware platform, violating the modularity goal. High diffusion: queries from Teleoperation, Safety Services, and NGHE would all reach into Vehicle.

Poor fit. Teleoperation is a human-in-the-loop interface, not a perception/planning backbone. The spatial database and feature extraction pipeline are not teleoperation concerns; they are autonomous decision-support infrastructure. Placing them here would couple human control logic to perception, and would require NGHE and Safety Services to query through Teleoperation, creating unnecessary indirection and diffusion.

Weak fit. Safety Services is a constraint-enforcement module, not a perception/planning backbone. While Safety Services will consume observations (via SafetyAlert), it should not own the spatial database or feature extraction pipeline. Doing so would couple safety logic to perception representation, and would force NGHE and Teleoperation to query Safety Services for observations, creating diffusion and violating separation of concerns.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the optimal boundary for containing both DP4 (spatial database and query layer) and DP5 (modular feature extraction pipeline). NGHE is the planning and perception backbone of the system; it is the natural consumer of spatial observations and the natural owner of the infrastructure that produces them. By centralizing the spatial database and feature extraction in NGHE, we achieve high internal cohesion (feature extraction feeds directly into planning queries) and low external diffusion (only four well-defined interfaces cross the boundary: Telemetry in, Tasking out, SafetyAlert out, Network Link inout). This design decouples sensor acquisition (Vehicle, Teleoperation) from representation and query (NGHE), allowing independent evolution of sensing strategies and planning/execution consumers.
containment_risks: NGHE Solution may become a monolithic perception-planning module if feature extraction pipelines are not carefully modularized internally. Mitigation: enforce internal separation between the feature extraction framework (pluggable extractors) and the spatial database/query layer (stable schema and query API).

If multiple remote planning nodes or edge devices need to query the spatial database, Network Link coupling may increase. Mitigation: design the Tasking interface to support both local and remote queries, and consider a publish-subscribe pattern for observation dissemination.

Safety Services may need real-time access to observations for constraint checking. If SafetyAlert latency becomes critical, consider a direct subscription pattern (SafetyAlert as a push interface) rather than polling. Current design supports this.

Feature extraction pipelines may require sensor-specific tuning (e.g., camera vs. lidar). If Vehicle or Teleoperation modules need to inject sensor-specific extractors, ensure the feature extraction framework in NGHE is truly pluggable and does not hardcode sensor assumptions.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: No revision to DP choice is necessary. The hybrid DP4 + DP5 is well-suited to NGHE Solution. However, to further reduce diffusion and improve modularity, consider explicitly decomposing the feature extraction pipeline into a sub-module within NGHE (e.g., 'Feature Extraction Framework') that is decoupled from the spatial database query layer via a well-defined tuple schema. This ensures that feature extractors can be added, removed, or modified without affecting the query layer, and vice versa. Additionally, ensure that Telemetry input is normalized at the NGHE boundary (not pushed deep into feature extractors), so that sensor-specific logic is isolated and the core pipeline remains agnostic to sensor type.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP4 + DP5 (hybrid spatial database + modular feature extraction pipeline) directly addresses FR (capture and represent worksite spatial features in machine-readable model). The tuple schema (geometry, class, confidence, timestamp, source) is feature-agnostic and machine-readable. Decoupling sensor acquisition from representation enables independent evolution of sensing strategies and planning consumers, satisfying the functional requirement. The design is logically sound and well-motivated.

diffusion_risks: Feature extraction pipeline modularity risk: if extractors are not cleanly separated from the spatial database query layer via the tuple schema, changes to extraction logic could cascade into query layer. Mitigation: enforce internal DP decomposition within NGHE (Feature Extraction Framework as sub-module with stable tuple output interface).

Telemetry input normalization: if sensor-specific preprocessing is pushed deep into feature extractors rather than normalized at the NGHE boundary, sensor coupling will diffuse into the core pipeline. Mitigation: establish a Telemetry adapter layer at NGHE boundary that normalizes raw observations before feeding the feature extraction framework.

Network Link coupling: if remote planning nodes or edge devices require direct spatial database queries, the Network Link interface may become a bottleneck or require complex synchronization logic. Mitigation: design Tasking interface to support both local and remote queries via publish-subscribe pattern; consider eventual consistency for distributed scenarios.

SafetyAlert latency: if Safety Services requires real-time hazard observations for constraint enforcement, polling the spatial database may introduce unacceptable latency. Current design supports push-based SafetyAlert, but implementation must ensure low-latency publication of high-confidence hazards.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the optimal IM boundary with 0.92 containment score and 0.08 diffusion score. The module owns the tuple schema, feature extraction framework, and query interfaces—all core to autonomous decision-making. Four well-defined external interfaces (Telemetry in, Tasking out, SafetyAlert out, Network Link inout) provide clear separation from Vehicle (hardware/actuation), Teleoperation (human interface), and Safety Services (constraint enforcement). Internal cohesion is high: feature extraction feeds directly into planning queries. No alternative module (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) provides better fit without severe coupling violations. Recommended internal decomposition: separate Feature Extraction Framework (pluggable extractors) from Spatial Database/Query Layer (stable schema and API) to further reduce diffusion and enable independent evolution.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Acceptance criterion 'A worksite model/map is generated and available to planning/execution functions for an agreed set of site features' is directly verifiable: (1) spatial database contains observations with geometry, class, confidence, timestamp, source; (2) Tasking interface exposes query API for planning/execution modules; (3) feature extraction pipeline produces tuples for agreed site features (obstacles, no-go zones, etc.). Verification can be automated via schema validation, query response testing, and feature coverage audits.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. NGHE Solution owns the spatial database and feature extraction pipeline (DP4 + DP5). Vehicle and Teleoperation provide raw sensor observations via Telemetry interface. Planning and execution modules query observations via Tasking interface. Safety Services consumes high-confidence hazards via SafetyAlert interface. Worksite safety plan and operational policies are enforced downstream by Safety Services and planning modules using observations from NGHE. Allocation is unambiguous and traceable from FR to DP to IM.

recommended_revisions: Explicitly decompose the feature extraction pipeline within NGHE into a sub-module (e.g., 'Feature Extraction Framework') that is decoupled from the spatial database query layer via the stable tuple schema (geometry, class, confidence, timestamp, source). This ensures extractors can be added, removed, or modified without affecting the query layer.

Establish a Telemetry adapter layer at the NGHE boundary that normalizes raw sensor observations (geometry, raw features, timestamps, source identifiers) before feeding the feature extraction framework. This isolates sensor-specific logic and keeps the core pipeline agnostic to sensor type (camera, lidar, etc.).

Design the Tasking interface to support both local (in-process) and remote (network) queries via a publish-subscribe pattern. This reduces coupling to Network Link and enables distributed planning scenarios without requiring direct database access from remote nodes.

Implement SafetyAlert as a push-based (event-driven) interface rather than polling. Ensure high-confidence hazard observations are published with low latency to Safety Services for real-time constraint enforcement.

Document the feature extraction framework's plugin architecture (e.g., extractor interface, registration mechanism, tuple schema contract) to ensure modularity and enable third-party or sensor-specific extractors without modifying core NGHE code.

Define acceptance criteria for 'agreed set of site features' explicitly (e.g., obstacles, no-go zones, traversable surfaces, landmarks). Create a feature registry in NGHE that maps feature types to extractors and validates that all required features are produced by the pipeline.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. The NGHE Solution is well-motivated, logically sound, and architecturally justified. However, the gate results explicitly recommend internal decomposition of the feature extraction pipeline and establishment of a Telemetry adapter layer to mitigate diffusion risks and ensure modularity. Given the high safety criticality of this worksite spatial modeling function, and the clear identification of sub-module boundaries (Feature Extraction Framework, Spatial Database/Query Layer, Telemetry Adapter), proceeding to next-level decomposition is justified to operationalize these mitigations and ensure implementation quality. The recommended revisions are not defects in the current design but rather opportunities to strengthen internal cohesion and reduce coupling at the next level of detail.
next_level_sub_frs: FR4.1: Normalize and validate raw sensor observations (geometry, raw features, timestamps, source identifiers) from heterogeneous telemetry sources (camera, lidar, radar, etc.) into a standardized observation format before feature extraction.

FR4.2: Extract and classify worksite features (obstacles, no-go zones, traversable surfaces, landmarks, hazards) from normalized observations using a pluggable, modular feature extraction framework that produces tuples conforming to the stable schema (geometry, class, confidence, timestamp, source).

FR4.3: Store, index, and query extracted feature observations in a spatial database with support for geometric queries, temporal filtering, and confidence-based ranking to enable planning and execution modules to retrieve relevant site features.

FR4.4: Publish high-confidence hazard observations (obstacles, no-go zones, safety-critical features) to Safety Services via a low-latency, event-driven SafetyAlert interface for real-time constraint enforcement.

FR4.5: Expose a Tasking interface that supports both local (in-process) and remote (network) queries to the spatial database via a publish-subscribe pattern, enabling distributed planning and execution without direct database coupling.

assumptions: Feature extraction pipeline can be cleanly decoupled from spatial database query layer via the stable tuple schema (geometry, class, confidence, timestamp, source).
Telemetry adapter layer can normalize sensor-specific preprocessing at the NGHE boundary without introducing unacceptable latency.
Pluggable feature extractor architecture (interface, registration, contract) is feasible and will not require core NGHE modifications for new extractors.
Publish-subscribe pattern for Tasking interface can support both local and remote queries without introducing synchronization bottlenecks or consistency violations.
SafetyAlert push-based (event-driven) publication can achieve low-latency delivery of high-confidence hazards to Safety Services.
'Agreed set of site features' can be explicitly defined and registered in NGHE (e.g., obstacles, no-go zones, traversable surfaces, landmarks) with clear mapping to extractors.

High safety criticality of worksite spatial modeling justifies investment in modular, well-documented internal architecture to reduce implementation risk and enable independent evolution of components.
open_risks: Feature extraction pipeline modularity: if extractors are tightly coupled to the spatial database query layer or to sensor-specific preprocessing, changes to extraction logic could cascade into downstream consumers. Mitigation: enforce internal DP decomposition within NGHE with stable tuple schema as contract.

Telemetry input normalization: if sensor-specific preprocessing is pushed deep into feature extractors rather than normalized at the NGHE boundary, sensor coupling will diffuse into the core pipeline. Mitigation: establish a Telemetry adapter layer at NGHE boundary.

Network Link coupling: if remote planning nodes require direct spatial database queries, the Network Link interface may become a bottleneck or require complex synchronization logic. Mitigation: design Tasking interface to support publish-subscribe pattern.

SafetyAlert latency: if Safety Services requires real-time hazard observations, polling the spatial database may introduce unacceptable latency. Mitigation: implement SafetyAlert as push-based (event-driven) interface with low-latency publication.

Feature registry completeness: if 'agreed set of site features' is not explicitly defined and validated, feature extraction pipeline may produce incomplete or inconsistent observations. Mitigation: create feature registry in NGHE with explicit acceptance criteria.

Distributed consistency: if remote planning nodes query the spatial database asynchronously, eventual consistency semantics must be clearly defined and acceptable to Safety Services and planning modules. Mitigation: document consistency guarantees and trade-offs in Tasking interface specification.
evidence_needed: Detailed specification of Telemetry adapter layer: input schema (raw sensor observations), output schema (normalized observations), supported sensor types, latency budget, and error handling.

Feature Extraction Framework architecture: extractor interface definition, registration mechanism, tuple schema contract, plugin discovery, and example extractors (obstacle detection, no-go zone classification, etc.).

Spatial Database/Query Layer specification: schema design (geometry, class, confidence, timestamp, source), indexing strategy, query API (geometric, temporal, confidence-based), and performance requirements.
Tasking interface specification: publish-subscribe pattern design, local vs. remote query semantics, consistency guarantees, latency budget, and error handling.

SafetyAlert interface specification: event schema (hazard type, geometry, confidence, timestamp), publication latency budget, subscription mechanism, and integration with Safety Services constraint enforcement.
Feature registry specification: feature type definitions (obstacles, no-go zones, traversable surfaces, landmarks, hazards), mapping to extractors, acceptance criteria, and validation mechanism.
Integration and acceptance testing plan: schema validation, query response testing, feature coverage audits, latency measurements, and distributed scenario testing.
Safety analysis: FMEA or similar for feature extraction pipeline, spatial database, and SafetyAlert interface to identify failure modes and mitigation strategies relevant to high safety criticality.
